revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]}} and {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Ono, Sokyo, ''Shinto: The Kami Way'', Charles E. Tuttle Company, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}}

It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]}} and {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Ono, Sokyo, ''Shinto: The Kami Way'', Charles E. Tuttle Company, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}}

File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}

[[en:Kamidana]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:كاميدانا]] {{Commons category|Kamidana}} [[cs:Kamidana]] [[de:Kamidana]] [[es:Kamidana]] [[fa:کامی‌دانا]] [[fi:Kamidana]] [[fr:Kamidana]] [[hy:Կամիդանա]] [[id:Kamidana]] [[it:Kamidana]] [[ja:神棚]] [[ko:가미다나]] [[no:Kamidana]] [[pl:Kamidana]] [[pt:Kamidana]] [[ru:Камидана]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kamidana]] [[tr:Kamidana]] [[uk:Камідана]] [[vi:Kamidana]] [[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{wikidata link|Q327584}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]}} and {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}

==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Ono, Sokyo, ''Shinto: The Kami Way'', Charles E. Tuttle Company, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}}

[[en:Kamidana]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:كاميدانا]] {{Commons category|Kamidana}} [[cs:Kamidana]] [[de:Kamidana]] [[es:Kamidana]] [[fa:کامی‌دانا]] [[fi:Kamidana]] [[fr:Kamidana]] [[hy:Կամիդանա]] [[id:Kamidana]] [[it:Kamidana]] [[ja:神棚]] [[ko:가미다나]] [[no:Kamidana]] [[pl:Kamidana]] [[pt:Kamidana]] [[ru:Камидана]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kamidana]] [[tr:Kamidana]] [[uk:Камідана]] [[vi:Kamidana]] [[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{wikidata link|Q327584}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]}} and {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Ono, Sokyo, ''Shinto: The Kami Way'', Charles E. Tuttle Company, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}}

It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}

(神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]}} and {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}

The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.

# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>

{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.

(神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]}} and {{transl|ja|[[:en:Shide (Shinto)|shide]]}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * [[:en:Etiquette in Japan|Etiquette in Japan]] * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Kamiza|Kamiza]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * [[:en:Spirit house|Spirit house]] * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}

{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>

{{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a [[:en:Spur|Spur]].}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s [[:en:Dojo|Dojo]]s.

However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[co:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[co:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[co:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاميدانا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kamidana]][[cs:Kamidana]][[de:Kamidana]][[en:Kamidana]][[es:Kamidana]][[fa:کامی‌دانا]][[fi:Kamidana]][[fr:Kamidana]][[hy:Կամիդանա]][[id:Kamidana]][[it:Kamidana]][[ja:神棚]][[ko:가미다나]][[no:Kamidana]][[pl:Kamidana]][[pt:Kamidana]][[ru:Камидана]][[simple:Kamidana]][[tr:Kamidana]][[uk:Камідана]][[vi:Kamidana]][[zh:神棚 (神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}}} and {{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a {{ill|Spur|en|Spur|WD=Q740732|lt=Spur}}.}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s {{ill|Dojo|en|Dojo|WD=Q203609|lt=Dojo}}s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|{{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan}}}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * {{ill|Etiquette in Japan|en|Etiquette in Japan|WD=Q634526|lt=Etiquette in Japan}} * {{transl|ja|{{ill|Kamiza|en|Kamiza|WD=Q1842153|lt=Kamiza}}}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * {{ill|Spirit house|en|Spirit house|WD=Q1442187|lt=Spirit house}} * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Ono, Sokyo, ''Shinto: The Kami Way'', Charles E. Tuttle Company, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}}

[[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}}} and {{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide They are most commonly found in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a {{ill|Spur|en|Spur|WD=Q740732|lt=Spur}}.}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.

==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>

(神道)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327584]] Wikidata name: kamidana [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Shinto altar}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kamidana.jpg|300px|right|thumb|A {{transl|ja|kamidana}} displaying a {{transl|ja|{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}}} and {{transl|ja|{{ill|Shide (Shinto)|en|Shide (Shinto)|WD=Q2657982|lt=shide}}}}]] {{nihongo3|{{lit|god/spirit-shelf}}|[[Wiktionary:神棚|神棚]]|'''Kamidana'''}} are miniature household altars provided to enshrine a [[Shinto]] {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}. They are most commonly found in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the home of {{transl|ja|kami}} worship.<ref>''Basic Terms of Shinto'', Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Tokyo 1985</ref>
The {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically placed high on a wall and contains a wide variety of items related to Shinto-style ceremonies, the most prominent of which is the {{transl|ja|[[shintai]]}}, an object meant to house a chosen {{transl|ja|kami}}, thus giving it a physical form to allow worship. {{transl|ja|Kamidana shintai}}{{efn|{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} also enshrine a {{transl|ja|shintai}}, which can however be of a kind not normally used for {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, for example a sword, a statue or a {{ill|Spur|en|Spur|WD=Q740732|lt=Spur}}.}} are most commonly small circular mirrors, though they can also be {{transl|ja|[[magatama]]}} jewels, or some other object with largely symbolic value. The {{transl|ja|kami}} within the {{transl|ja|shintai}} is often the deity of the local shrine or one particular to the house owner's profession. A part of the {{transl|ja|kami}} ({{transl|ja|[[bunrei]]}}) was obtained specifically for that purpose from a shrine through a process called {{transl|ja|[[kanjō]]}}.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto">{{cite book |last=Bocking |first=Brian |date=1997 |title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto |location=Lincolnwood, Illinois |publisher=NTC Publishing |isbn=9780844204253 |oclc=36977290}}</ref> Worship at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}} typically consists of the offering of simple prayers, food (e.g., rice, fruit, water) and flowers.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Before worshipping at the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, it is ritually important for family members to cleanse their hands or mouth.
{{transl|ja|Kamidana}} can also be found in some traditional [[:en:Japanese martial art|Japanese martial art]]s {{ill|Dojo|en|Dojo|WD=Q203609|lt=Dojo}}s.
==Acquisition and care== A household {{transl|ja|kamidana}} is typically set up in one's home to enshrine an {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, a type of charm. Both {{transl|ja|kamidana}} and {{transl|ja|ofuda}} can be obtained at any large {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}. {{transl|ja|Ofuda}} by themselves can be displayed on a counter or anywhere visible, provided that they are kept in their protective pouches. However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>

However, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, there are several rules which must be followed to ensure proper installation.
# First, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up on the ground or at eye level. It must be above an ordinary person's eye level. # Second, a {{transl|ja|kamidana}} cannot be set up over an entrance; it must be built into a space which people will not walk under. # Finally, when an {{transl|ja|ofuda}} is enshrined in a {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, after removing the pouch it is customary to leave an offering of water, [[Omiki|liquor]], or food in front of the {{transl|ja|kamidana}}, which should be renewed regularly.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> Water, for example, is stored in a small, droplet-shaped vessel called a {{transl|ja|mizutama}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://library.artstor.org/#/asset/SS36617_36617_32702751;prevRouteTS=1543259656534|title=Artstor|last=Artstor|website=library.artstor.org|language=en|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britishmuseum.org/research/collection_online/collection_object_details.aspx?objectId=3800341&partId=1&images=true|title=altar|website=British Museum|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-11-26}}</ref>
These rules apply both to one's household and to martial arts dojos.
{{transl|ja|Ofuda}} are replaced before the end of each year.<ref name="Popular Dictionary of Shinto" /> However, {{transl|ja|kamidana}} can be kept in one's house until they are no longer usable.
==Examples== <gallery> File:Kamidana2.jpg|alt=A small pinewood shrine, featuring a plain torii gate decorated with streamers, a small mirror in front of a cup, and a blue daruma doll.|A personal {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Kamidana with kagamimochi offering by shig2006 in Hitachinaka.jpg|alt=A large, darkwood altar, featuring paper and wooden sticks with kanji tied together with mizuhiki cord, behind two stacked rice cakes with a satsuma balanced on top.|A larger household {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Toei Uzumasa-0347.jpg|alt=A large, decorative alcove shrine, with red paper lanterns decorated with black text strung across the top, a miniature torii gate in front and two red daruma dolls at either side.|A decorative {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}}. File:Name-the-baby&household-Shinto-altar,japan.JPG|alt=A wooden household shrine featuring two tōrō lanterns made of wood, and a piece of paper announcing the name of a baby.|A {{transliteration|ja|kamidana}} celebrating the naming of a baby. File:Sacred straw rope at New Year's,shimenawa,katori-city,japan.jpg|{{transliteration|ja|Kamidana}} with {{transliteration|ja|[[Jingū taima|Jingu Taima]]}} </gallery>
==See also== * {{transl|ja|{{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan}}}} – analogous concept in Japanese Buddhism * {{ill|Etiquette in Japan|en|Etiquette in Japan|WD=Q634526|lt=Etiquette in Japan}} * {{transl|ja|{{ill|Kamiza|en|Kamiza|WD=Q1842153|lt=Kamiza}}}} * {{transl|ja|[[Ofuda]]}} * {{ill|Spirit house|en|Spirit house|WD=Q1442187|lt=Spirit house}} * {{nihongo3|{{lit|{{transl|ja|tama}}, "soul [of the dead]" + {{transl|ja|ya}}, "house"}}|霊屋|[[:en:Tamaya|Tamaya]]}} - also called {{transl|ja|otamaya}}, {{transl|ja|mitamaya}}, or {{transl|ja|soreisha}} * {{transl|ja|[[Tokonoma]]}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Ono, Sokyo, ''Shinto: The Kami Way'', Charles E. Tuttle Company, {{ISBN|4-8053-0189-9}}
